title: TypeScript Type System: Deep Cuts
description: Narrowing you can trust, the `any`/`unknown`/`never`/`void` lattice, structural-typing surprises, and literal widening with `as const` & `satisfies`. Every explanation compiles the snippet so a wrong answer becomes a lesson.

questions.title: After this `typeof` guard, what is the type of `x`?
questions.explanation:
`typeof null` is the string `"object"` â€” a famous JavaScript wart that TypeScript faithfully models. So `typeof x === "object"` is true for the array **and** for `null`, while `string` (whose `typeof` is `"string"`) is excluded. The branch narrows to `string[] | null`, which means `x.length` here would error with *'x' is possibly null*.

To exclude `null`, guard on truthiness first: `if (x && typeof x === "object")`, or `if (Array.isArray(x))`.

questions.title: Which guard narrows `Fish | Bird` by checking for a property?
questions.explanation:
The `in` operator is a narrowing type guard: `"swim" in animal` tells the compiler the object has a `swim` property, so the true branch narrows to `Fish` and the `else` to `Bird`.

The distractors fail for concrete reasons:

- `animal.swim !== undefined` doesn't type-check â€” you can't read `.swim` off the union before narrowing.
- `typeof animal === "Fish"` is impossible: `typeof` only ever yields runtime tags like `"object"`, never a type name.
- `instanceof Fish` needs `Fish` to be a class/constructor value; here it's only a *type*, which doesn't exist at runtime.

questions.title: Why is `val` still `unknown` inside the `if`?

options.text: The return type is `boolean`, not a type predicate `x is string`, so the call carries no narrowing information.
`unknown` can never be narrowed by a function call.
`typeof x === "string"` doesn't work on a parameter typed `unknown`.
You must annotate `val` as `any` for the guard to take effect.

questions.title: Why does `token.length` error inside the listener?

options.text: `token` is reassigned in scope, so the compiler discards the narrowing inside the closure â€” the callback may run after `token` becomes `null`.
Arrow functions never inherit narrowed types from their enclosing scope.
`addEventListener` strips the narrowed type from any captured variable.
`!== null` doesn't narrow `string | null`; you must use `typeof`.

questions.explanation:
Control-flow narrowing is a fact about a *point in the code*. A closure runs later, so the compiler only trusts narrowing inside it when the variable can't change â€” i.e. when it's `const` or an effectively-never-reassigned `let`/parameter. Here `token = null` later in scope makes `token` reassignable, so inside the callback its type reverts to the declared `string | null`.

Proof: **delete the `token = null` line** and the error disappears â€” `token` becomes effectively const and the narrowing survives into the closure. Robust fixes: copy to a `const` (`const t = token; ... t.length`), or assert at point of use.

questions.title: What forces a compile error when a new union member is forgotten?

options.text: In `default`, `shape` is narrowed to `never` only if every case is handled; a new member leaves a real type that won't assign to `never`.
The `switch` statement requires a `default` case to be exhaustive.
`never` makes the function throw at runtime when an unknown shape arrives.
Assigning to `_exhaustive` re-runs the discriminated-union check at runtime.

questions.explanation:
After the `circle` and `square` cases, the discriminated union is fully consumed, so in `default` TypeScript narrows `shape` to `never`. Assigning `never` to a `never`-typed variable is fine.

Now add `| { kind: "triangle"; b: number; h: number }` to `Shape` but forget its `case`. In `default`, `shape` narrows to the *triangle* type â€” which is **not** assignable to `never` â€” so `const _exhaustive: never = shape` fails to compile, pointing at exactly the missing case. This is a purely compile-time safety net; the `never` type produces no runtime behavior on its own.

questions.title: Which line fails to compile?
questions.explanation:
`any` *opts out* of type checking: every property access and call on `a` is allowed (and unchecked), so Line 1 compiles. `unknown` is the type-safe counterpart â€” it accepts any value but permits **no** operations until you narrow it, so `u.foo` errors with *'u' is of type 'unknown'*.

The takeaway: prefer `unknown` for values of uncertain shape (API responses, `JSON.parse`, caught errors). You're then forced to prove the shape before use:

```ts
if (typeof u === "object" && u !== null && "foo" in u) {
  u.foo; // now allowed
}
```

questions.title: Which type is the type-safe *top type* of TypeScript?
questions.description: The top type accepts a value of any other type, yet permits no operations on it until you narrow.
questions.explanation: Every type is assignable **to** `unknown`, which makes it the top of the lattice â€” but unlike `any`, you can't *do* anything with an `unknown` until you narrow it, so safety is preserved. `any` also accepts everything, yet it additionally lets you perform any operation (and is assignable *back* to every type), so it sits outside the sound lattice rather than at its top. `never` is the opposite end â€” the **bottom** type, assignable to everything but inhabited by no value. `object` only accepts non-primitives, so `42` or `"hi"` wouldn't assign to it.

questions.title: Does this compile?

options.text: Yes â€” a function that returns a value is assignable to a `void`-returning callback type; the return value is simply ignored.
No â€” `push` returns `number`, which is not assignable to the callback's `void` return type.
Yes, but only because `out` is typed `number[]` rather than `unknown[]`.
No â€” you must wrap the body in braces so the arrow returns `undefined`.

questions.explanation:
A `void` return type means *"the caller will ignore whatever this returns"* â€” not *"this must return nothing."* So a function returning any type is assignable to a `() => void` parameter. This deliberate rule is what lets the ubiquitous `arr.forEach(x => out.push(x))` compile despite `push` returning a number.

Because the value is contractually ignored, the *result* of calling such a callback is still typed `void`:

```ts
type VoidFn = () => void;
const f: VoidFn = () => 42; // OK
const r = f();             // r: void â€” the 42 is unreachable through this type
```
questions.references: [TypeScript Handbook: Assignability of functions â€” return type `void`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/functions.html#return-type-void)

questions.title: Is the assignment to `name` a type error?

options.text: No â€” `never` is the bottom type and is assignable to every type, including `string`.
No, and it's also fine at runtime since `never` resolves to `undefined`.
Yes â€” `never` cannot be assigned to `string`.
No type error â€” but `name` is never actually assigned, because `fail` throws before returning.

questions.explanation:
Both true statements matter, so read the options carefully. The assignment **type-checks** precisely because `never` is the bottom type: it's assignable to every type, so `const name: string = fail(...)` is legal. But the most complete answer also captures runtime reality â€” `fail` has return type `never` exactly because it *can't* return normally (it always throws), so control never reaches the binding of `name`.

The option claiming a type error is wrong (that would be true of `unknown`, not `never`), and `never` has no runtime value at all â€” it does not 'resolve to `undefined`'.

questions.title: Reduce the intersection: what single type is `string & number`?
questions.description: Fill in the result of `type T = string & number;` â€” answer with one lowercase word.
questions.explanation:
An intersection `A & B` is the set of values that are **both** an `A` and a `B`. No value is simultaneously a `string` and a `number`, so the set is empty â€” and the type of the empty set is `never`. This is one of the quiet places `never` appears without you writing it:

```ts
type T = string & number;     // never
type U = "a" & "b";          // never
type V = { x: 1 } & { x: 2 };  // x: never
```

The same logic powers exhaustiveness checks, where narrowing away every union member leaves `never`.

questions.title: Where does `never` (not `any`) silently show up?
questions.description: Select every snippet whose described type is `never`.

options.text: The return type of a function whose body only ever `throw`s.
The discriminant's type in the `default` of a fully exhaustive `switch`.
The inferred type of `let x;` declared with no initializer.

questions.explanation:
`never` is the type with no values, so it surfaces wherever a value is impossible:

- A function that always `throw`s never returns a value â†’ return type `never`.
- Once a discriminated union is fully narrowed, nothing is left for `default`, so the discriminant is `never` (the basis of exhaustiveness checks).

The other two are classic **`any`** sources, not `never`:

- `let x;` with no initializer is an *evolving* `any` (it adapts to the first assignment).
- `JSON.parse` is declared to return `any`, which is exactly why wrapping it to return `unknown` is a popular safety upgrade.

questions.title: Which assignment errors on the extra `z` property?
questions.explanation:
TypeScript is *structural*: a value is assignable if it has at least the required members. By that rule, `{ x, y, z }` is a perfectly good `Point`. So why does Line A error?

**Excess-property checks** are an extra guard that fires only when a **fresh object literal** is assigned (or passed) directly to a typed target â€” TypeScript assumes a literal with surprise properties is a typo or a misread API, and flags `z`. Line B launders the very same object through a variable (`tmp`), so it's no longer 'fresh': only the structural rule applies, `tmp` has `x` and `y`, and it assigns cleanly. Other escape hatches: an `as Point` assertion, or giving `Point` an index signature.

questions.title: Does `const p: Point = v;` compile?

options.text: Yes â€” `Point` and `Vector` have identical shapes, so they're interchangeable regardless of their names.
No â€” they are distinct named types and `Vector` does not `implements Point`.
No â€” interfaces are nominal in TypeScript; only classes are structural.
Yes, but only after an explicit `as Point` assertion.

questions.explanation:
TypeScript's type system is **structural**, not nominal: compatibility is decided by shape, not by name or declared relationship. `Point` and `Vector` expose exactly the same members, so each is assignable to the other with no `implements`, no inheritance, and no cast. (This applies to classes too â€” a class instance fits any interface whose members it satisfies.)

When you genuinely want two same-shaped types to be *incompatible*, you must opt into nominal typing yourself â€” see branded types in the next question.

questions.title: Why does a one-argument callback satisfy `map`?
questions.description:
```ts
// map's callback type is (value: number, index: number, array: number[]) => U
const doubled = [1, 2, 3].map((x) => x * 2); // one parameter â€” no error
```

options.text: A function with fewer parameters is assignable to a function type with more â€” extra arguments may be ignored.
`map` special-cases single-parameter callbacks at the type level.
TypeScript auto-fills the missing `index` and `array` parameters as `undefined`.
It only works because the array's element type is `number`.

questions.explanation:
Function-type compatibility allows the *source* function to declare **fewer** parameters than the *target* expects â€” a caller is always free to pass extra arguments a function ignores. So `(x) => x * 2` is assignable where `(value, index, array) => U` is wanted; `map` still passes all three at runtime, the callback just doesn't name the last two.

This mirrors everyday JavaScript (`['a','b'].forEach(console.log)` would pass index and array to `console.log` too) and is why you rarely spell out `index`/`array` unless you need them.

questions.title: Why does `getUser(raw)` fail but `getUser(raw as UserId)` succeed?

options.text: The brand adds a phantom property that no plain `string` has, so `string` isn't assignable to `UserId` without an explicit assertion.
`UserId` is a nominal type keyword that disables structural typing for strings.
`__brand` must be assigned a real value at runtime before the call type-checks.
Template-literal types reject any string that isn't a known literal.

questions.explanation:
A **branded type** intersects a base type with a unique, fictitious member (`{ readonly __brand: "UserId" }`). Structurally, an ordinary `string` lacks that `__brand` property, so it isn't assignable to `UserId` â€” this manufactures *nominal* behavior on top of a structural system, so you can't accidentally pass any old string where a validated `UserId` is required.

The brand exists only in the type world; `__brand` is never actually present at runtime. You mint a `UserId` deliberately â€” typically inside a validating factory that ends with `return value as UserId` â€” which is why the explicit assertion in the second call is the intended escape hatch.

questions.title: What is the type of `config.mode`?
questions.explanation:
`const` freezes the *binding* `config`, not the object it points to. Because `config.mode` can still be reassigned (`config.mode = "light"`), TypeScript **widens** the property to `string` â€” the literal `"dark"` would be too restrictive for a mutable slot.

Contrast a top-level primitive `const`, where the binding can't change so the literal is kept: `const mode = "dark"` has type `"dark"`. To preserve the literal *inside* an object, add `as const`:

```ts
const config = { mode: "dark" } as const; // mode: "dark" (readonly)
```

questions.title: What are the inferred types of `a` and `b`?
questions.explanation:
A `const` binding to a string literal can never be reassigned, so TypeScript infers the narrow **literal type** `"hello"`. A `let` binding can be reassigned to any other string, so the compiler *widens* the inferred type to `string` up front.

This is why `const` is often the better default for building precise types â€” and why values destined for a literal union frequently need either `const`, an explicit annotation, or `as const` to keep from widening.

questions.title: What is the type of `pair`?
questions.explanation:
Without the assertion, `const pair = [3, 4]` infers `number[]` â€” a mutable array of unknown length. A **`const` assertion** does three things at once: it narrows each element to its literal type (`3`, `4`), turns the array into a fixed-length **tuple**, and marks the whole thing `readonly`. The result is `readonly [3, 4]`.

That exact, frozen shape is what makes `as const` the idiom for config objects, action-type unions, and anything you'll feed to `keyof typeof` or a literal-union derivation.

questions.title: Which keeps the precise inferred type *and* validates against the constraint?
questions.explanation:
The three tools differ in *which direction* they trade type precision for checking:

- **A) Annotation** validates the value, but then `theme` *is* `Record<string, Color>`: `theme.primary` widens to `Color` (you lose `"blue"`), and `theme.anything` is allowed. Checked, but imprecise.
- **C) `as` assertion** performs no real validation â€” a typo like `{ primary: "bleu" }` slips through â€” and it overrides the inferred type. Precise-looking, but unsafe.
- **B) `satisfies`** checks the literal against `Record<string, Color>` (so `"bleu"` is rejected) **without** changing the inferred type. `theme` stays `{ primary: "blue" }`, so `theme.primary` is the literal `"blue"`. The constraint also acts as a contextual type, which is what stops `"blue"` from widening to `string`.

`satisfies` (TypeScript 4.9+) is the modern answer to *"validate the shape, but let me keep the narrow types."*

questions.title: Which assertion makes `[3, 4]` infer as a readonly tuple `readonly [3, 4]`?
questions.description: Answer with the two-word TypeScript assertion you append to the literal.
questions.explanation:
Appending **`as const`** applies a *const assertion*: literals keep their narrowest types, arrays become `readonly` tuples, and object properties become `readonly`. So `[3, 4] as const` is typed `readonly [3, 4]` instead of the widened `number[]`.

Unlike a normal `as T` cast (which changes the type to *exactly* `T`), `as const` doesn't name a target type â€” it just tells the compiler to infer the most specific, immutable type possible.
